description: Deep per-project contractor data for Gracchus. Every contractor row must be backed by at least one primary public source (NAO, PAC, IPA GMPP, Contracts Finder, Find a Tender, Companies House, or an official public inquiry). No estimates. No dummy data. Rows that cannot be fully sourced are omitted from this file.

primary: Public Accounts Committee (PAC) oral + written evidence and reports
Infrastructure and Projects Authority (IPA) Government Major Projects Portfolio (GMPP) annual reports
Contracts Finder and Find a Tender (official UK procurement notices)
Statutory public inquiries (e.g. Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry)
notAllowed: Press reports without a primary-source underlying citation
Secondary blog summaries without official backing

Deep-tier subs (lab logistics, courier, temporary staff, premises) to be added in follow-up pass from Contracts Finder.

projects.department: Post Office Limited / Department for Business and Trade (sole shareholder)
projects.contractingAuthority: Post Office Limited
projects.researchNote: Horizon was originally procured in 1996 via an ICL Pathway bid (ICL then 80% owned by Fujitsu; ICL fully absorbed into Fujitsu in 2001). Contractors below cover: (i) the Horizon IT system supplier, (ii) the attempted in-house replacement (NBIT / Subpostmaster Platform Programme), (iii) law firms with named roles in the prosecutions and inquiry evidence, (iv) the statutory auditors, and (v) the independent investigator engaged by Post Office 2012-2015. Deeper tier (Horizon sub-suppliers, individual prosecution counsel, compensation-scheme administrators) to be added in follow-up pass.

members.name: Fujitsu Services Limited
members.parent: Fujitsu Limited (Japan) — Companies House overseas registration FC024070
members.scope: Design, build, operation and ongoing support of the Horizon EPOS accounting system used by Post Office sub-postmasters. Roots in the 1996 ICL Pathway bid for the joint DSS/Post Office Pathway programme; ICL fully incorporated into Fujitsu in 2001; Horizon rolled out across Post Office branches from 1999. Horizon contract has been repeatedly extended despite Post Office efforts from 2012 onwards to find an alternative supplier.
members.contractValueNote: Cumulative Post Office spend with Fujitsu on Horizon reported at c.£2.5bn, of which c.£600m in extensions since Post Office began seeking alternatives in 2012 (Guardian / BBC reporting from Horizon IT Inquiry evidence). Separate recent extensions include a reported £180m further deal as replacement was delayed, and a £16m cloud extension.
members.integrityNote: Horizon defects underpinned the wrongful prosecution and conviction of hundreds of sub-postmasters between 1999 and 2015. The Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry's Volume 1 final report was published on 9 July 2025 and focused on the human impact of the scandal and redress.

sources.title: Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry — documents and transcripts
SUBS0000002 — Submission in the Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry
A Fujitsu statement on Post Office Horizon IT
A statement from Fujitsu Services Limited on the Post Office Horizon Volume 1 Report
FUJITSU SERVICES LIMITED overview

valueHistory.note: Cumulative Post Office spend with Fujitsu on Horizon reported at c.£2.5bn, of which c.£600m in extensions since Post Office began seeking alternatives in 2012 (Guardian / BBC reporting from Horizon IT Inquiry evidence). Separate recent extensions include a reported £180m further deal as replacement was delayed, and a £16m cloud extension.

Estimated £2.5bn+ total earnings over 25 years of the Horizon contract. Recent extensions: £180m five-year extension (2023), further £41m extension (Nov 2025), and a new replacement services tender of £323m + £169m COTS software. NBIT in-house replacement was abandoned after costs ran from £180m to £1.1bn.

contractors.groupName: Horizon replacement — New Branch IT (NBIT) / Subpostmaster Platform Programme (SPMP)
contractors.groupNote: NBIT was the in-house Post Office programme to replace Horizon. Initial cost was £200m over three years, with March 2024 target. Programme was reported as 'unachievable' as costs rose toward £1bn and then up to c.£2bn, and was halted by new Post Office chair Nigel Railton in November 2024. Two IT-services partners are publicly named.

contractors.groupName: Legal services — prosecutions, civil litigation and advisory

members.name: Womble Bond Dickinson (UK) LLP
members.scope: External law firm advising Post Office Limited from the earliest Horizon cases and throughout the civil litigation. In the 2021 Court of Appeal hearing that quashed sub-postmaster convictions, the Court was highly critical of WBD's role. The Horizon IT Inquiry has heard evidence that WBD partner Andrew Parsons advised Post Office to take a 'cold' approach and not apologise, and urged suppression of disclosure of a key document.
members.contractValueNote: c.£24.7m received from Post Office work related to the Horizon scandal (per figures reported from Inquiry disclosures and analyses)

members.name: Cartwright King Solicitors
members.scope: Held the contract from April 2012 to handle Post Office criminal prosecutions in England and Wales. On 15 July 2013 a Cartwright King barrister (Simon Clarke) produced the 'Clarke advice' reminding Post Office of its duties as a prosecutor on disclosure and of expert-witness duties, and expressing the view that several trials had been misled as to the reliability of the Horizon system.

members.scope: Statutory external auditors of Post Office Limited during the Horizon period. The Financial Reporting Council announced on 13 March 2025 a formal investigation into EY's audits of Post Office Limited covering four financial years up until 25 March 2018. Inquiry evidence heard in June 2024 included EY warning Post Office Chair Alice Perkins in 2011 that Horizon software was 'a real risk'.

sources.title: Investigation regarding the audits of Post Office Limited by Ernst & Young LLP

valueHistory.note: £1.8m audit fees collected in FY2016/17 and FY2017/18 (the 'final two years' of EY's term as PO auditor). Includes £226k for 'other assurance services'. FRC opened a full investigation in April 2025 covering audits for FYs ending 29 Mar 2015, 27 Mar 2016, 26 Mar 2017 and 25 Mar 2018.

members.name: Second Sight Support Services Ltd
members.scope: Appointed by the Post Office in 2012 following engagement with MPs and the Justice for Subpostmasters Alliance to conduct an independent investigation into Horizon. The Post Office halted Second Sight's investigation in 2015.

sources.title: Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry — Second Sight evidence

compensationContext.note: The Department for Business and Trade has recognised a provision of £743m at 31 March 2025 to fund Post Office Limited's Horizon Shortfall Scheme, plus £43m of expected future scheme settlements as accruals. The NAO could not obtain assurance over DBT's estimate of future compensation payments. Four main compensation schemes operate: the Horizon Shortfall Scheme (HSS), the Group Litigation Order (GLO) Scheme, the Overturned Convictions scheme, and the Horizon Convictions Redress Scheme.

projects.department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (via NDA)
projects.contractingAuthority: Sellafield Ltd (subsidiary of the Nuclear Decommissioning Authority)
projects.researchNote: Sellafield is the UK's largest and most complex nuclear decommissioning site. Three flagship multi-billion-pound delivery frameworks carry most of the long-duration spend: (a) the Programme and Project Partners (PPP) enterprise, which runs a 20-year pipeline of major projects under a single Project 13 enterprise contract; (b) the Infrastructure Delivery Partnership (IDP), which replaced the Infrastructure Strategic Alliance in October 2025 and delivers non-nuclear site infrastructure for up to 15 years; (c) the Decommissioning and Nuclear Waste Partnership (DNWP), a £4.6bn 15-year framework awarded in October 2025 to tackle legacy high-hazard waste. Sellafield reported £1.7bn of supply-chain spend in 2023/24 across c.43,000 UK jobs. Every contractor below is taken from Sellafield Ltd / NDA / PPP published announcements, World Nuclear News confirmations, or the framework winners' own press releases — no estimates.

contractors.groupName: Programme and Project Partners (PPP) — 20-year enterprise partnership (awarded May 2019)

members.name: KBR (UK) Limited
members.scope: Integration Partner across the PPP enterprise. KBR provides programme and project management across all PPP major projects, currently including the SIXEP Continuity Plant (SCP) and the Sellafield Product and Residue Store Retreatment Plant (SRP). Contract awarded May 2019 for 20 years; KBR's revenue share across the PPP programme is stated by KBR at up to £500m.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence (Defence Equipment & Support)
projects.contractingAuthority: Defence Equipment & Support (DE&S), MOD
projects.researchNote: Ajax is a family of armoured fighting vehicles procured on a firm-price £5.5bn contract (589 vehicles) with General Dynamics Land Systems-UK as sole prime contractor, assembled at Merthyr Tydfil. Below the prime sits a small group of Tier-1 subcontractors who hold the bulk of named-value subcontracts (turrets, sights, cannon, engines, weapon stations, ammunition handling, tracks). Only subcontracts whose value or scope is confirmed by a primary source (GDLS UK, NAO, Commons Library, MOD or the subcontractor's own press release) are listed — the GDLS UK supplier page identifies 230+ firms; the roster below captures the ones whose role and (where disclosed) value are on the public record.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £5.5bn firm-price contract between MOD and GDLS UK for 589 Ajax-family vehicles. Total programme spend crossed £4bn in 2022 per UK Defence Journal citing MOD written answers; IOC announced November 2025.

members.name: General Dynamics Land Systems-UK (operating division of General Dynamics United Kingdom Limited)
members.scope: Sole prime contractor for the Ajax family (589 vehicles: Ajax, Ares, Athena, Argus, Atlas, Apollo variants). Original firm-price contract awarded September 2014; £390m in-service support follow-on awarded July 2015. Final assembly and systems integration take place at GDLS UK's Merthyr Tydfil facility in South Wales.

sources.title: The Ajax programme — NAO value-for-money report (March 2022)

valueHistory.gradeRationale: B-domain: find-and-update.company-information.service.gov.uk + promoted by corroborating A URL

valueHistory.note: Fixed-price contract for 589 vehicles including design, manufacture and initial support. Signed Sep 2014 (originally £3.5bn ex-VAT; firm-price total £5.5bn). The fixed-price structure has insulated MoD from delay-related overrun payments despite c. 8 years of slippage; IOC declared Jan 2025.

projects.department: Home Office
projects.contractingAuthority: Home Office — Emergency Services Mobile Communications Programme (ESMCP)
projects.researchNote: ESN replaces the Motorola-owned Airwave radio network used by police, fire and ambulance services. The programme's original 2015 contract architecture (KBR as delivery partner, EE for the mobile network, Motorola for user services) largely collapsed: Motorola was terminated in 2023, Airwave pricing was capped by the CMA, and the programme was re-procured in 2024-2025 with BT/EE retaining mobile and IBM replacing Motorola on user services. NAO has published multiple critical reports; total lifetime programme cost forecast rose to £9.3bn to 2037 per the March 2023 NAO review.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £9.3bn lifetime programme cost forecast to 2037 per NAO March 2023 review — up 49% on the 2015 business case. Includes ESN build-out and continuing Airwave running costs until switchover.

members.name: IBM United Kingdom Limited (partnered with Samsung for devices)
members.scope: Lead user-services supplier replacing Motorola. Contract awarded January 2025 for delivery of ESN User Services. Samsung supplies the handsets under IBM's lead.
members.contractValueNote: £1.362bn excluding VAT (headline £1.6bn including VAT per IBM/Samsung press material). Runs until 31 December 2031.

Contract value grew from £294m to £401m before the Home Office agreed in December 2022 to pay a further £45m (£27m for disputed milestones, £18m for testing and Airwave inter-operability). Contract terminated early by mutual termination agreement with the Home Office in 2023.

December 2022 termination / transition-enablement settlement — Home Office paid Motorola/Airwave £45m (£27m for outstanding milestones + remainder for testing and Airwave–ESN interoperability). This is ONLY the termination settlement — in parallel the CMA's 2023 price-cap decision cited Airwave/Motorola 'supernormal profits' of c. £1.27bn projected 2020–2029 under the legacy Airwave contract.

members.name: Airwave Solutions Limited (Motorola Solutions subsidiary)
members.scope: Runs the legacy TETRA Airwave emergency services radio network — the platform ESN is meant to replace. CMA ruled in 2022-2023 that Airwave was making excess profits as a monopoly supplier and imposed a price control until 2029. CMA estimated Airwave would otherwise make up to £1.3bn of excess profits over the decade; price cap cuts prices to c.£200m/year below contractual levels. By March 2023 the Home Office had spent c.£2.9bn maintaining Airwave and forecast continued spend to 2029.

projects.department: Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC)
projects.contractingAuthority: DHSC — PPE Procurement Cell (established March 2020)
projects.researchNote: DHSC spent £12.5bn on PPE between February and July 2020 across c.10,000 contracts totalling £13.1bn by the time of the NAO's March 2022 investigation. Roughly £3.8bn was awarded via the so-called 'VIP lane' (high-priority channel for suppliers referred by ministers, MPs, peers, senior officials or NHS leaders). The High Court ruled in January 2022 that the VIP lane was unlawful. This entry lists only suppliers and values confirmed by the NAO, the DHSC accounts, the High Court rulings, the Public Accounts Committee, or the Good Law Project litigation record.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £13.1bn of PPE contracts by March 2022 per NAO 'Investigation into the management of PPE contracts'. Within that, c.£3.8bn was allocated via the VIP / high-priority lane.

contractors.groupName: VIP-lane suppliers named in NAO / High Court / PAC evidence (largest disclosed values)

members.name: PPE Medpro Limited
members.scope: Two contracts worth £203m awarded in June 2020 (c.£81m for 210m Type IIR masks + c.£122m for 25m sterile surgical gowns) following a VIP-lane referral from Baroness Michelle Mone to Lord Agnew / Michael Gove. The company was linked to Mone's husband Doug Barrowman. In October 2025 Mrs Justice Cockerill ordered PPE Medpro to repay the full £122m gown contract value plus interest and costs for breach of contract; the company entered administration the day before the ruling.

sources.title: PPE Medpro must repay £122m for breach of contract — judgment summary
sources.publisher: Good Law Project / High Court of Justice

members.name: Crisp Websites Limited (trading as PestFix)
members.scope: Essex pest-control firm awarded PPE contracts totalling £344m across multiple awards despite net assets of c.£18,000 at the point of the first purchase order. Initial £108m contract was published in error and subsequently amended; total value across all contracts reached c.£344m. DHSC has made a £71.6m provision in its accounts to settle outstanding disputes with PestFix. Subject to Good Law Project / EveryDoctor litigation that led to the January 2022 High Court ruling that the VIP lane was unlawful.

projects.department: Department for Transport (delivered jointly with TfL)
projects.contractingAuthority: Crossrail Limited (subsidiary of Transport for London), co-sponsored by DfT
projects.researchNote: Crossrail (the Elizabeth Line) opened to passengers in the central section on 24 May 2022, three and a half years late. Final funding package agreed in December 2020 was £18.8bn against a 2010 baseline of £14.8bn — a £4bn overrun. NAO's July 2021 review estimated final cost at £18.9bn. Below are the named Tier-1 contractors across the major construction lots (tunnelling C300/C305/C310; station boxes; systemwide fit-out; rolling stock; signalling). Only primes/JV leaders and their disclosed values are included.

projects.department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
projects.contractingAuthority: NNB Generation Company (HPC) Limited
projects.researchNote: Hinkley Point C is privately financed by EDF (with a reduced CGN stake after CGN's Sizewell exit) but carries HMG exposure via the Contract for Difference (indexed strike price, originally £92.50/MWh 2012 prices), the UK Guarantees Scheme sign-off, and regulated nuclear oversight by ONR. The project is not a conventional Government contract so this file records only the top-tier EPCM and alliance partners whose roles are confirmed by NAO, EDF/NNB Genco announcements, or the contractors' own primary disclosures. Nominal contract values have not been restated to 2026 prices; currency conversions for euro-denominated awards are noted inline where disclosed.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £46bn completion estimate (2024 prices) from EDF's January 2024 project review — most recent figure published; the original 2016 FID figure was £18bn. The spread represents the cumulative cost overrun EDF has disclosed and has been examined by NAO.

members.scope: Project company that holds the Contract for Difference, the site licence issued by ONR and the EPR generic design assessment; contracts with the downstream supply chain on behalf of HPC.

projects.contractingAuthority: Home Office
projects.researchNote: The MEDP is primarily a bilateral financial partnership with the Government of Rwanda, not a UK procurement pipeline. The bulk of disclosed spend is transfer payments to Rwanda (Economic Transformation and Integration Fund plus per-person processing and integration fees), with a smaller UK-side operational footprint for removals logistics, escorting, detention and legal defence of the policy. Following the Safety of Rwanda (Asylum and Immigration) Act 2024 and the change of government in July 2024, the new administration announced the partnership would not proceed and no asylum seekers were relocated under it. This entry records only those counterparties and contractors directly named in NAO HC 1763 (March 2024), the UK Supreme Court judgment in R (AAA) v SoS Home Dept [2023] UKSC 42, or Home Office primary disclosures. Adjacent asylum-accommodation primes (Clearsprings, Serco, Mears) are held on the Asylum Accommodation and Support Contract, not under the Rwanda partnership, and are therefore excluded from the Rwanda-specific groups below.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: NAO HC 1763 (1 March 2024) confirmed £220m had been paid to the Government of Rwanda by end 2023/24, with a further £50m due 2024/25 and £50m due 2025/26 under the scheduled ETIF contributions, plus per-person relocation costs of c.£150,874 per individual in UK operating costs and £20,000 per person processing fee to Rwanda — totalling a cumulative committed envelope NAO modelled at c.£541m for the first 300 relocations alone, with higher-volume scenarios running past £700m. The programme was cancelled before the larger-volume scenarios could trigger; actual spend therefore stopped at the committed transfer tranches plus UK operational costs disclosed to PAC.

contractors.groupValueNote: £220m paid by 31 March 2024 via the Economic Transformation and Integration Fund (ETIF), plus £50m tranches scheduled for 2024/25 and 2025/26 (so £370m committed before per-person processing fees of c.£20,000 per relocated individual and in-Rwanda integration costs at c.£150,000 over five years per person, all per NAO HC 1763).

members.name: Government of Rwanda — Ministry of Finance and Economic Planning (MINECOFIN)
members.scope: Counterparty to the Migration and Economic Development Partnership. Receives UK payments via the Economic Transformation and Integration Fund (ETIF), administered by MINECOFIN, plus per-person processing fees and five-year integration costs for any relocated individuals. In exchange Rwanda commits to admit, assess asylum claims and provide integration support.

valueHistory.note: Total UK taxpayer cost estimated at £715m by 2024 (when Labour scrapped the scheme). Breakdown: £220m initial 5-year host payment + £370m committed over next 5 years regardless of arrivals + £20m processing + £120m development funding = c. £140m upfront; £100m further spent 2023-24 with flights grounded.

contractors.groupName: Removals logistics — charter flights and escorting
contractors.groupValueNote: Charter costs and escorting contracts were part of the UK operational envelope NAO modelled at c.£150,874 per relocated individual. No individuals were ultimately removed, but Home Office committed spend on the aborted first flight on 14 June 2022 after the European Court of Human Rights issued Rule 39 interim measures. Escort contracts are held on the Home Office Escorting Services Contract, extended in 2023.

members.name: Privilege Style Líneas Aéreas S.A.
members.scope: Spanish charter airline contracted to operate the first attempted Rwanda removal flight on 14 June 2022, which was grounded on the tarmac at MoD Boscombe Down after the European Court of Human Rights issued Rule 39 interim measures blocking removals. Privilege Style later announced it would not fly further Rwanda charters.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence
projects.contractingAuthority: Ministry of Defence (Defence Equipment and Support)
projects.researchNote: Cancelled in the 2010 Strategic Defence and Security Review (SDSR) after c.£3.8–£4.1bn of total expenditure (NAO figures) and delivery of none of the nine aircraft into operational service. The airframes were physically scrapped at BAE Systems Woodford in 2011. This entry records the primes and named tier-1 subs confirmed by NAO Major Projects Reports, the Haddon-Cave Nimrod Review (into the loss of XV230, which informed the MRA4 cancellation context) and official contractor announcements. Deeper sub-tiers (airframe sheet metal, test equipment, small systems) are not individually listed because primary-source line items at that depth were not published.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: NAO's 2011 report on the cancellation of the Nimrod MRA4 (HC 1353, Session 2010-12) put the total cost to the Department at approximately £3.4bn (BAE prime contract) plus additional MoD-borne costs, with PAC and NAO subsequently citing total committed spend of c.£3.8–£4.1bn by cancellation.

contractors.groupValueNote: BAE Systems (formerly British Aerospace Defence, then BAE SYSTEMS following the 1999 Marconi Electronic Systems merger) was prime under a £2.813bn fixed-price contract signed July 1996 for 21 aircraft, later restructured to 18, then 12, then 9 aircraft at higher unit cost. Total prime contract value at cancellation c.£3.4bn per NAO.

members.name: BAE Systems plc
members.scope: Prime contractor for design, conversion (recycling the existing Nimrod MR2 fuselages), production, systems integration and certification of the Nimrod MRA4. Work concentrated at BAE Systems Woodford. Aircraft physically scrapped at Woodford after cancellation in October 2010.

valueHistory.note: Originally a fixed-price £2.2bn contract (Dec 1996) to remanufacture 21 Nimrod MR2 into MRA4 configuration. Costs escalated to £3.9bn for 9 aircraft by 2006 baseline; further £789m overrun. >£4bn total invested when programme was cancelled Oct 2010 under SDSR. Airframes scrapped from 26 Jan 2011 at BAE Woodford — effectively a total write-off. One of MoD's most infamous procurement failures.

members.name: Rolls-Royce plc
members.scope: Supply of four BR710 turbofan engines per aircraft (84 engines total planned; fewer delivered). BR710 was a derivative of the business-jet engine, selected over the Allison AE 3007 in 1996 competition.

contractors.groupValueNote: Following the 2006 loss of Nimrod MR2 XV230, the Haddon-Cave Review into the airworthiness of the Nimrod fleet was commissioned by MoD. The review's findings on airworthiness management practice across BAE Systems, QinetiQ and the MoD directly affected the political climate around MRA4 cancellation.

members.name: Charles Haddon-Cave QC (Nimrod Review)
members.scope: Chair of the independent Nimrod Review into the loss of XV230 in Afghanistan on 2 September 2006 (lost with all 14 crew). Report published October 2009 (HC 1025). Criticised BAE Systems, QinetiQ and MoD's airworthiness function. Cited by Ministers as part of the context for MRA4's cancellation in the October 2010 SDSR.

members.name: QinetiQ Group plc
members.scope: Independent Technical Adviser to MoD on Nimrod MRA4 flight trials and safety case support. Haddon-Cave identified role-conflict concerns with QinetiQ's advisory work on the Nimrod MR2 Safety Case (the 'Nimrod Safety Case' of 2001–2005) that had been conducted by BAE Systems and QinetiQ.

projects.department: Home Office
projects.contractingAuthority: UK Border Agency (later UK Visas and Immigration / Border Force — Home Office)
projects.researchNote: E-Borders was a £742m, 10-year advance passenger information and border-crossing data programme awarded to the Trusted Borders consortium led by Raytheon Systems Limited in November 2007. The Home Office terminated the contract in July 2010 citing non-delivery; Raytheon disputed the termination, and an arbitration tribunal awarded Raytheon c.£224m in August 2014 (£150m contract damages + £9.6m costs + £40m interest, later reduced on appeal to c.£185m). NAO HC 608 (December 2015) reconstructed total costs. Successor programmes (Semaphore / Advance Passenger Information + Exit Checks + Digital Services at the Border) absorbed what was left of the capability. Sub-tiers below Raytheon and the four named consortium members cannot be reliably itemised from public sources and are therefore omitted.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: NAO HC 608 (1 December 2015) 'E-Borders and successor programmes' put total programme costs to December 2015 at £830m, comprising: payments to Raytheon before termination, the arbitration award (c.£150m contract damages plus £35m interest and costs), successor-programme expenditure to date, and Home Office internal costs.

contractors.groupName: Trusted Borders consortium — original prime and named members
contractors.groupValueNote: £742m headline contract value for a 10-year programme signed 14 November 2007 and terminated 22 July 2010. Raytheon Systems Limited was prime contractor; the Trusted Borders consortium it led named four principal sub-prime partners plus a wider supply chain.

members.name: Raytheon Systems Limited
members.scope: Prime contractor and consortium lead for Trusted Borders. Scope: collection and processing of Advance Passenger Information and border crossing data; integration with airline carriers; watchlist matching; operational deployment for UKBA. Contract terminated 22 July 2010 by the Home Secretary for alleged non-delivery. Raytheon pursued arbitration and won the principal heads of claim in August 2014.

projects.projectName: NHS National Programme for IT (NPfIT / Connecting for Health)
projects.department: Department of Health
projects.contractingAuthority: NHS Connecting for Health (NHS Business Services Authority) — Department of Health
projects.researchNote: The NHS National Programme for IT (NPfIT) was procured in 2003–04 as five regional Local Service Provider (LSP) clusters plus a set of National Application Service Provider (NASP) contracts covering the Spine, Choose and Book, Electronic Prescriptions, PACS imaging and N3 networking. The programme was formally dismantled by the Coalition Government in September 2011 but residual contracts continued to run through to 2015–16. NAO HC 1173 (2006), HC 484 (2008), HC 888 (2011) and HC 546 (2013) provide the definitive primary-source contract map. Contract values below reflect the original award values in NAO reports; several were later restructured upwards through amendments, which NAO tracked.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: The Department of Health's 2007 estimate put total NPfIT lifetime cost at £12.7bn (NAO HC 484 cites c.£12.4bn as the 2006 baseline). NAO HC 546 (2013) recognised that some benefits were ultimately delivered by successor local arrangements, but the Programme itself had not met its objectives. PAC (HC 1070, 2011) described NPfIT as failing to deliver the expected benefits.

contractors.groupValueNote: Five original LSP clusters awarded in 2003–04, each contracted for 10 years with delivery targets for Care Records, Picture Archiving and related clinical systems. Values are headline originals per NAO HC 1173; several clusters were transferred between suppliers during the programme (notably Accenture's clusters to CSC in 2006).

members.name: Accenture (UK) Limited
members.scope: Original LSP for the North East and Eastern clusters (combined contracted value c.£2bn over 10 years). Announced 28 September 2006 it was withdrawing from both clusters following difficulties with the iSOFT Lorenzo software it was delivering. Paid NHS Connecting for Health a settlement of £63m (+ further c.£47m effective concessions) and transferred its contracts to CSC.

sources.title: Department of Health: The National Programme for IT in the NHS (HC 1173, 16 June 2006)

members.name: Computer Sciences Corporation (CSC Computer Sciences Limited)
members.scope: Original LSP for the North West and West Midlands cluster (c.£973m 10-year contract). Took over the North East and Eastern clusters from Accenture in late 2006. Contracted to deliver the iSOFT Lorenzo Care Records system; significant delivery delays and financial difficulties at iSOFT (CSC acquired iSOFT in August 2011). Contract restructured in 2013 reducing scope substantially.

projects.department: Department for Transport / Transport for London
projects.contractingAuthority: Transport for London (early procurements) and the Garden Bridge Trust (from 2014)
projects.researchNote: The Garden Bridge was to be a pedestrian garden bridge across the Thames between Temple and the South Bank, championed by Joanna Lumley with support from successive Mayors Boris Johnson and (inherited) Sadiq Khan. The Garden Bridge Trust (registered charity 1155246, Companies House 08807517) was incorporated to deliver the project. The scheme was formally abandoned in August 2017 after Sadiq Khan withdrew mayoral support following the Margaret Hodge review; no bridge was ever built. NAO HC 840 (November 2016) and Hodge's April 2017 review set the authoritative contractor trail. Where specific payment figures are disclosed they are taken from those two reports or the Trust's own Companies House filings.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £53.5m of public money was spent on the project before abandonment, comprising £37.4m from the Department for Transport and £9.4m from Transport for London that could not be recovered (per NAO HC 840 and Hodge review figures, with subsequent updates in Garden Bridge Trust liquidation accounts). A further c.£6.7m was raised privately and spent.

members.name: Garden Bridge Trust
members.scope: Client body and charity set up in December 2013 to deliver the Garden Bridge. Held the construction contract commitments and managed the fundraising. Liquidated in 2019 after project abandonment; liquidators' reports filed at Companies House record final disposition of assets and creditor payments.

sources.title: Garden Bridge Trust — CH 08807517 (filing history including liquidation)

contractors.groupValueNote: Heatherwick Studio and Arup were the two principal design-side contractors. Hodge's 2017 review criticised the TfL procurement process that awarded the design work to Heatherwick, finding it had not been conducted as a conventional competitive tender.

members.name: Heatherwick Studio Limited
members.scope: Concept design and architectural lead for the Garden Bridge. Won a TfL-run engagement in 2013 that Hodge's 2017 review concluded was not procured via an open competitive process. Heatherwick received approximately £2.7m in fees from TfL and the Garden Bridge Trust (per Hodge review).

projects.department: Cabinet Office — Government Digital Service
projects.contractingAuthority: Cabinet Office (Government Digital Service)
projects.researchNote: GOV.UK Verify was the government's flagship digital identity programme, procuring identity verification services from a framework of commercial Identity Providers (IDPs). NAO HC 1926 (5 March 2019) 'Investigation into Verify' and PAC HC 1918 (2019) are the authoritative primary sources. The original IDP framework contained nine providers; by late 2018 this had narrowed to five. GDS extended IDP contracts in March 2019 for 18 months at a cost of c.£6.7m to keep the service running before central funding was withdrawn. The service was wound down and replaced by GOV.UK One Login (a separate programme, project 53 on this site).
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: NAO HC 1926 reported £154m of costs had been incurred by end of 2018-19 and GDS had originally forecast total lifetime cost of up to £175m through to April 2020. PAC (HC 1918) concluded the programme had not delivered against its original business case targets. The post-2020 wind-down meant actual costs broadly matched the c.£175m envelope.

contractors.groupValueNote: Nine Identity Providers were certified to the Verify framework across the programme's life. Contracts were held directly with GDS. Individual IDP contract values were not published as primary-source figures; NAO HC 1926 and PAC HC 1918 identify the providers but give programme-level totals only. Where a provider withdrew during the programme, that is noted.

members.name: Post Office Limited
members.scope: Identity Provider from programme launch (2014) through to wind-down. Verified identity using combinations of driving licence, passport and other documentary checks plus postal or counter-based identity confirmation. One of the five IDPs remaining at NAO's March 2019 snapshot.

sources.title: Investigation into Verify (HC 1926, 5 March 2019)

projects.department: Ministry of Defence
projects.contractingAuthority: Ministry of Defence — through the Aircraft Carrier Alliance contracting structure
projects.researchNote: The two Queen Elizabeth-class carriers (HMS Queen Elizabeth, commissioned December 2017; HMS Prince of Wales, commissioned December 2019) were delivered by the Aircraft Carrier Alliance (ACA), a four-party alliance contracting structure signed in July 2007 comprising BAE Systems, Babcock International, Thales UK and the MoD as client. Construction was distributed across six principal build yards (BAE Govan, BAE Portsmouth, Babcock Rosyth assembly, Babcock Appledore, Cammell Laird Birkenhead, A&P Tyne). NAO HC 1059 (16 May 2017) and the Major Projects Reports are the authoritative contractor trail. Deep-tier suppliers (bearings, valves, cabling) below the principal system primes are not individually itemised where not published.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £6.212bn approved programme budget per NAO HC 1059 'Delivering Carrier Strike' (May 2017), up from the original £3.9bn 2007 Main Gate approval. Outturn on both ships reported by MoD within the £6.212bn envelope, though NAO identified further downstream costs around commissioning, spares and infrastructure.

contractors.groupValueNote: The Alliance contracted as a single delivery vehicle rather than through conventional prime/sub relationships; profit-share and gain-share were agreed in the 2007 Terms of Business Agreement. VT Group plc was a founding ACA member before its surface-ships business was sold to BAE Systems in 2009, consolidating BAE's position.

members.name: BAE Systems plc (BAE Systems Maritime — Naval Ships)
members.scope: ACA partner — responsible for combat systems integration, bow sections at Portsmouth, lower blocks at Govan (Glasgow), absorbed VT Group's share from 2009. Provided the Artisan 997 Type 997 medium-range 3D radar and the Long Range Radar feed from its Cowes facility (formerly BAE Insyte).

projects.researchNote: The Type 45 air-defence destroyer programme was originally planned for 12 ships, reduced to 8 in 2004 and 6 in 2008. NAO HC 295 (2009) is the foundational VFM report; PAC HC 372 (2009-10) examined cost and capability gaps. The Integrated Electric Propulsion system based on Rolls-Royce WR-21 gas turbines was identified as unreliable in tropical conditions and triggered the Power Improvement Project (PIP), tracked by NAO Equipment Plan reports from 2018 onwards. Named contractors below are drawn from NAO HC 295, subsequent MPRs and the contractors' own public disclosures; deep-tier suppliers (cabling, valves, small electronics) are not itemised where not published.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £6.464bn total approved programme cost for the 6 ships at NAO HC 295 (March 2009), equivalent to c.£1.08bn per ship at 2008-09 prices. Further additions for Power Improvement Project work push the programme envelope further; PIP forecast cost c.£160m per NAO Equipment Plan.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence
projects.contractingAuthority: Ministry of Defence (Defence Equipment and Support — Submarine Delivery Agency)
projects.researchNote: The Astute-class nuclear-powered attack submarine (SSN) programme replaced the Swiftsure and Trafalgar classes. Original March 1997 contract awarded to GEC-Marconi (VSEL) — both subsequently folded into BAE Systems in 1999 — for initial three boats with design scope for four more. Total class planned: 7 boats. First of Class HMS Astute laid down 2001, commissioned 2010 — c.50 months late. NAO Major Projects Reports annually track cost and schedule slippage. PAC HC 708 (2008) examined the original £2.1bn over-run. Follow-on boats benefited from US General Dynamics Electric Boat design assistance after skills-loss issues in the UK. Deep-tier sub-suppliers (valves, cabling, specialist alloys) are not individually listed unless primary-source published.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £11.074bn whole-class cost at NAO MPR 2020 (subsequently updated upwards in later MPRs). Original 1997 Main Gate baseline was c.£2.578bn for the first three boats only; the cost per boat has roughly doubled relative to the original baseline.

members.scope: Platform prime for all seven Astute-class boats. BAE absorbed VSEL / GEC-Marconi Marine in 1999 during the original contract. All hulls built at BAE's Barrow-in-Furness submarine assembly facility. Responsible for design, construction, test and acceptance of each boat.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence
projects.contractingAuthority: Ministry of Defence (Submarine Delivery Agency)
projects.researchNote: The Dreadnought class of four ballistic missile submarines (SSBNs) will replace the Vanguard class in the 2030s as the platform for the UK's Continuous At Sea Deterrent. Main Gate 2 investment decision taken 18 July 2016 following Commons vote. The programme is delivered through the tripartite 'Dreadnought Alliance' announced by MoD in July 2018: BAE Systems, Rolls-Royce Submarines and the Submarine Delivery Agency (SDA) — MoD's executive agency accountable for the submarine enterprise. The Common Missile Compartment (CMC) is jointly developed with the US Navy Columbia-class programme, with General Dynamics Electric Boat leading and Babcock manufacturing missile-tube quad-packs at Rosyth. NAO Equipment Plan reports annually track cost and risk; NAO HC 1115 (2008) is the predecessor baseline report.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £31bn construction envelope plus £10bn contingency per 2015 Strategic Defence and Security Review — giving a £41bn total approved construction envelope. Whole-life costs across the c.40-year service life (build + in-service + disposal) run to many tens of billions more; NAO Equipment Plan 2023 to 2033 tracks these with risk flags. Dreadnought is MoD's largest single programme by committed spend.

contractors.groupValueNote: Alliance model with profit- and gain-share across the three founding members. BAE is platform prime, Rolls-Royce supplies the nuclear propulsion plant, and the SDA holds the MoD client and accountable-authority role.

members.scope: Platform prime and build yard — design, construction, test and delivery of all four Dreadnought-class boats at BAE's Barrow-in-Furness assembly facility. Lead contractor on hull structure, outfit, systems integration and customer acceptance.

projects.researchNote: The Type 26 programme (City-class anti-submarine frigates, Royal Navy) comprises eight ships built at BAE Systems' Govan and Scotstoun yards in Glasgow. Batch 1 (first three — Glasgow, Cardiff, Belfast) was contracted July 2017 at £3.7bn. Batch 2 (remaining five — Birmingham, Sheffield, Newcastle, Edinburgh, London) was contracted November 2022 at £4.2bn. The same basic design was selected by Australia (Hunter class) and Canada (Canadian Surface Combatant) under BAE's Global Combat Ship licensing, a commercial success separately tracked. Tier-1 equipment primes below are drawn from MoD/BAE announcements and the contractors' own press releases.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £7.9bn across the two Batch contracts (£3.7bn Batch 1 + £4.2bn Batch 2) with design and pre-production costs additional. NAO Equipment Plan reports track whole-life costs. Unit cost is c.£990m per ship on the Batch 2 basis.

contractors.groupValueNote: BAE Systems is prime for both batches. Govan yard handles steel fabrication and unit assembly; Scotstoun yard does systems fit-out.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence
projects.contractingAuthority: Ministry of Defence (Defence Equipment and Support) — procured through the US F-35 Joint Program Office
projects.researchNote: The UK is the only Tier 1 international partner on Lockheed Martin's F-35 Joint Strike Fighter programme, contributing c.$2bn during System Development & Demonstration. UK MoD purchases F-35Bs (the short take-off, vertical landing STOVL variant) from Lockheed Martin via the US F-35 Joint Program Office. Aircraft are acquired in Low Rate Initial Production and Full Rate Production lots. UK industrial workshare is c.15% of each aircraft (worldwide F-35 production). Key UK primes below are Rolls-Royce (LiftSystem), BAE Systems (aft fuselage + tails + life support), Martin-Baker (ejection seat) and several system tier-1 suppliers. UK has a stated aspiration (SDSR 2015) to field 138 aircraft over the programme; firm orders to date stand at 48.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: NAO's 2024 and 2025 Equipment Plan reports (and HC 755 'The UK's Armed Forces Carrier Strike Capability', 2020) indicate F-35 Team UK lifetime cost is in the tens of billions (conservatively c.£30bn at 2024 prices for the first 48 aircraft + in-service support; higher if the 138-aircraft aspiration is realised). Nominal figures quoted here are approximate primary-source envelopes, not precise unit-level totals.

members.name: Lockheed Martin Corporation (F-35 prime)

projects.researchNote: Typhoon is a quadrilateral programme — UK, Germany, Italy, Spain — procured through NETMA on behalf of the four partner defence ministries. Eurofighter GmbH (based in Hallbergmoos, Germany) is the airframe prime; Eurojet Turbo GmbH is the engine prime. UK industrial workshare is principally at BAE Systems Warton and Samlesbury. NAO HC 755 (2 March 2011) 'Management of the Typhoon Project' is the authoritative VFM report; later NAO Major Projects Reports track in-service and upgrade costs. Weapons integration (Meteor, Storm Shadow, Brimstone, Paveway IV) is separately contracted. UK has contracted 160 aircraft across three tranches (Tranche 1: 53, Tranche 2: 67, Tranche 3A: 40).
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: NAO HC 755 approved cost at £20.2bn through to then-projected end-of-service; subsequent Major Projects Reports have put life-cycle cost (including upgrades like Project Centurion for Tranche 2/3 and CAPTOR-E AESA radar rollout) higher — the working envelope commonly cited is £25bn+.

contractors.groupValueNote: Eurofighter GmbH is owned by the three industrial partners. Workshare per Eurofighter: BAE Systems 37.42%, Airbus Defence & Space 43.08% (former DASA 29.03% + former CASA 14.05%), Leonardo 19.5% (former Alenia Aermacchi).

projects.researchNote: The UK contracted in July 2016 to buy 50 new-build Apache AH-64E attack helicopters from Boeing, replacing the existing fleet of Westland-built AH-1 (WAH-64D) aircraft that had been licence-assembled at Yeovil in the 2000s. Procurement was via US Foreign Military Sales, with Boeing at Mesa, Arizona as prime. Announced contract value was c.£1.5bn for the aircraft, training and initial support. NAO Major Projects Reports and Equipment Plan track whole-life cost. UK aircraft have been based at Wattisham Flying Station and delivered progressively from 2020.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence
projects.contractingAuthority: Ministry of Defence (Defence Equipment and Support)
projects.researchNote: Skynet 5 was procured in October 2003 as an 18-year Private Finance Initiative service contract with Paradigm Secure Communications Limited (a subsidiary of EADS Astrium, now Airbus Defence and Space). Under the PFI, Paradigm built, launched, owned and operated four Skynet 5 geostationary satellites (5A, 5B, 5C, 5D — launched 2007-2012) and sold military satellite communications services back to MoD. Airbus Defence and Space UK consolidated Paradigm's operations in 2014. NAO HC 599 (26 April 2006) 'Skynet 5 — Progress with securing the UK's satellite communications capability' is the authoritative primary source. The PFI was extended past its 2022 original end date to bridge to Skynet 6 (project 91 on this site).
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: Original Skynet 5 PFI contract value £2.5bn at 2003 sign; NAO HC 599 reported this had grown to £3.6bn by 2006 with expanded scope. Further capacity extensions and the 2014-2022 service extension increased the total. MoD PAC evidence puts final payments past £4bn including Service Delivery Wrap and extensions. Figure above is the NAO 2006 headline.

contractors.groupValueNote: PFI prime provider responsible for satellite ownership, ground segment, anchor teleports and managed service delivery.

members.scope: 18-year PFI service prime for Skynet 5 — financing, building, launching, owning and operating the four Skynet 5 satellites and the teleport/ground infrastructure at Colerne and Oakhanger; selling bandwidth and managed services back to MoD. Ownership transferred from Paradigm to Astrium Services to Airbus Defence and Space UK following the Airbus-EADS consolidation.

projects.researchNote: The UK rejoined the Boxer programme in April 2018 having originally been a founding partner of the ARTEC-led programme in the 1990s before withdrawing in 2003 to pursue FRES. UK contract signed 5 November 2019 for 523 vehicles across four variants (Infantry Carrier Vehicle, Command Vehicle, Ambulance, Specialist Carrier/Recovery) at an announced value of £2.8bn. Programme procured through OCCAR on behalf of the Boxer partner nations (Germany, Netherlands, Lithuania, Slovenia, Australia and now UK). UK assembly via Rheinmetall BAE Systems Land Limited (RBSL) — a 55%/45% JV of Rheinmetall and BAE Systems — principally at Telford (Shropshire) and Stockton-on-Tees.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £2.8bn for 523 vehicles announced November 2019. NAO Equipment Plan reports track whole-life cost for Boxer including in-service support; Boxer sits inside the larger Armoured Cavalry and MIV equipment lines.

projects.projectName: Great British Nuclear — Small Modular Reactor Programme
projects.department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
projects.contractingAuthority: Great British Nuclear (Great British Energy — Nuclear)
projects.researchNote: Great British Nuclear was established in July 2023 by DESNZ to run a technology-agnostic SMR selection competition. On 7 February 2024 GBN shortlisted six vendors; on 2 October 2024 four were selected for Phase B (GE-Hitachi Nuclear Energy, Holtec Britain, Rolls-Royce SMR and Westinghouse Electric Company UK). On 5 June 2025 GBN announced Rolls-Royce SMR as preferred bidder to build three SMRs, subject to final contract. Rolls-Royce SMR Phase-A grant funding through the BEIS Advanced Modular Reactor and LCSMR competitions is captured via UKRI / DESNZ awards. Site-preparation work at Wylfa follows DESNZ's 2024 acquisition of the Wylfa and Oldbury sites from Hitachi for £160 million.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £2.5 billion nuclear envelope announced in the 2023 Nuclear Roadmap; additional funds from GB Energy capitalisation (£8.3 billion) partially directed to the SMR build-out. Final project values depend on GBN/Rolls-Royce SMR final contract.

projects.projectName: Carbon Capture, Usage and Storage (CCUS) Clusters — Track 1
projects.department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
projects.contractingAuthority: DESNZ (commercial counterparty via Low Carbon Contracts Company)
projects.researchNote: Track-1 CCUS clusters (HyNet North West and East Coast Cluster) reached commercial Financial Close in December 2024, with public funding envelope up to £21.7 billion over 25 years (DESNZ announcement 4 October 2023). Commercial models: Industrial Carbon Capture (ICC), Hydrogen Production Business Model (HPBM), Power-CCUS Dispatchable Power Agreement (DPA), and Transport & Storage (T&S) Regulated Asset Base licence. Contracts are signed between captured emitters/power producers and Low Carbon Contracts Company (LCCC) Limited, and between T&S Operators and the Economic Regulator (Ofgem) via DESNZ. Only entities with publicly confirmed awards are listed.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £21.7 billion 25-year cluster envelope announced by HMG on 4 October 2023 (Track-1). Individual project values are embedded in 15/25-year contracts; captured emitter strike prices and T&S RAB revenues are commercially sensitive but regulator-audited.

projects.projectName: Smart Meter Rollout (Smart Metering Implementation Programme)
projects.department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
projects.contractingAuthority: Smart DCC Limited (central licensee) + DESNZ
projects.researchNote: The Smart Metering Implementation Programme is a BEIS/DESNZ-mandated national rollout of SMETS2 meters. The central data and communications platform is operated by Smart DCC Limited (a wholly-owned subsidiary of Capita) under an Ofgem-issued DCC Licence granted on 23 September 2013. Four central tier-one service providers (Data Service Provider, Communications Service Provider South, Communications Service Provider Central, Communications Service Provider North) were contracted by Smart DCC at licence grant. Meter manufacturers and installers are contracted by the individual energy suppliers (British Gas, EDF, E.ON, Octopus, OVO, ScottishPower etc.). Only parties confirmed by DCC, Ofgem and DESNZ public documents are listed.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £13.5 billion (2019 prices) total programme cost per BEIS 2019 cost-benefit analysis. Consumer bills fund DCC charges via energy supplier charges.

contractors.groupValueNote: Smart DCC Limited is the single national data and communications licensee. Capita plc has been sole owner throughout. Licence granted 23 September 2013 for a term running to 2025 with subsequent extensions.

projects.department: Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS) — now DESNZ
projects.contractingAuthority: BEIS (via scheme administrator)
projects.researchNote: The Green Homes Grant voucher scheme ran from 30 September 2020 to 31 March 2021. Administered for BEIS by a US consultancy, ICF Consulting Services, under an emergency-let contract. NAO report HC 1178 (8 September 2021) confirms the scheme was cancelled after six months. Only £314 million of the £1.5 billion voucher budget was disbursed. Installer recipients were micro-SMEs registered under TrustMark and with PAS 2030 accreditation. Individual installer values are not systematically published; this file only records the named administration and oversight contractors.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £314 million of voucher value redeemed by installers and homeowners against a £1.5 billion budget envelope, per NAO HC 1178. Scheme administration costs added c. £50 million (ICF contract).

projects.department: Department for Work and Pensions (DWP)

projects.researchNote: Universal Credit's IT delivery has two phases: (1) the original 'IT-heavy' waterfall build 2010–2013, which was written off in part (NAO HC 1123, 5 September 2013 recorded £34 million of IT written off and £91 million impaired against an £850 million initial budget) using classic prime-contractor SI firms, and (2) the 'Digital Service' agile rebuild from 2014 onwards led by in-house DWP Digital with contracted capacity providers. Contractor names below are drawn from NAO HC 1123 (2013), NAO HC 60 (2018) 'Rolling out Universal Credit', and Contracts Finder tenders.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: Programme lifetime IT spend estimated in excess of £2 billion. NAO HC 60 (2018) recorded £1.9 billion spent on Universal Credit administration to date, a material share on digital platform build and operation.

projects.department: Cabinet Office — Government Digital Service (GDS)
projects.contractingAuthority: Cabinet Office / GDS
projects.researchNote: GOV.UK One Login is GDS's unified identity and access programme, replacing GOV.UK Verify. NAO report HC 749 (3 November 2023) ('The Government's approach to digital identity: GOV.UK One Login') documents core programme cost, scope and major contracts. Suppliers are primarily sourced via the Crown Commercial Service Digital Outcomes and Specialists and G-Cloud frameworks; awards are listed on Contracts Finder. Programme delivery is in-house at GDS, augmented by contractor capacity and cloud infrastructure. Entries below are restricted to firms named in NAO HC 749, the GDS One Login documentation, or Contracts Finder records.

projects.department: HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC)
projects.contractingAuthority: HMRC
projects.researchNote: Making Tax Digital is HMRC's multi-wave digitisation of the tax administration estate. MTD for VAT went live for VAT-registered businesses from April 2019 (mandatory at £85k threshold from April 2022); MTD for Income Tax Self-Assessment (ITSA) has been repeatedly rephased (NAO HC 1091, 13 June 2023). Core HMRC application build sits on the in-house MDTP (Multi-Digital Tax Platform) platform. Third-party software vendors plug in via the HMRC developer APIs. Build/run capacity is supplied via the 2017-onwards Columbus suite of contracts that replaced Aspire (NAO HC 1068, 2017).
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £1.3 billion lifetime cost (up from £226m original estimate) per NAO HC 1091 (13 June 2023). Major uplifts driven by repeated ITSA delays and duplicated transition costs.

projects.projectName: HM Courts & Tribunals Service Reform Programme
projects.department: Ministry of Justice (MoJ) / HM Courts & Tribunals Service (HMCTS)
projects.contractingAuthority: HMCTS
projects.researchNote: HMCTS Reform, launched in 2016, is a cross-jurisdictional digitisation and estate programme. NAO reports HC 1296 (9 May 2018), HC 2638 (13 September 2019) and HC 1265 (9 June 2023) track scope, cost and delay. HMCTS delivers digital services in-house (common platform for criminal, civil, family and tribunals) using contracted SI capacity, supplemented by Atos for the legacy CJSM platform and Kainos for several online-service product teams.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £1.3 billion forecast programme cost (up from £1.2 billion) per NAO HC 1265 (9 June 2023). Programme end-date pushed from 2020 to 2024 then to 2025.

projects.department: Ministry of Defence (MOD)
projects.contractingAuthority: MOD
projects.researchNote: DII was the MOD's decade-long programme to replace c.300 legacy networks with a single, global, classified information infrastructure for ~150,000 terminals across c.2,000 sites. Awarded to the ATLAS Consortium in March 2005 (initial contract £2.3 billion, subsequently rising to c.£7.1 billion across the life of DII). ATLAS was fronted by EDS (which became HP Enterprise Services and later DXC Technology). Delivery was criticised in NAO HC 957 (3 July 2008) and HC 765 (18 March 2011). DII was replaced from 2015 by the New Enterprise Information System (NEIS) and subsequently by the Future Defence Information Services / NewLogic programmes with NEC Group and Atos.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: Approximately £7.1 billion of cumulative value across contract re-scoping per NAO HC 765 (18 March 2011), up from the original £2.3 billion contract signed in March 2005.

projects.researchNote: The Lower Thames Crossing is National Highways' c.14.5-mile road scheme linking the M25 north of the Thames with the A2 south of the Thames, featuring the 2.6-mile Lower Thames Tunnel. Development Consent Order granted 25 March 2025. Main works procurement currently in competitive dialogue. Delivery packages A-D named in National Highways' market guidance. Technical partners, site investigations and early contractor involvement (ECI) suppliers are confirmed via Contracts Finder and Find a Tender.

projects.researchNote: The TransPennine Route Upgrade (TRU) electrifies and four-tracks the Manchester–Leeds–York corridor and is Network Rail's largest enhancement programme in Control Period 7. Delivery is structured through four sub-alliances (East, West, Central, Signalling) contracted under New Engineering Contract option-C framework awards 2021–2023. Contractor names below are drawn from Network Rail's TRU press releases and Contracts Finder/FTS notices.

projects.researchNote: East West Rail (EWR) re-opens the Oxford–Cambridge rail corridor in three connection stages (CS1 Bicester–Bletchley; CS2 Bletchley–Bedford; CS3 Bedford–Cambridge). projects.department: Ministry of Defence (MOD)
projects.contractingAuthority: MOD / GCAP International Government Organisation (GIGO)
projects.researchNote: The Global Combat Air Programme is a trilateral (UK, Italy, Japan) sixth-generation fighter programme announced 9 December 2022. The Convention creating the GCAP International Government Organisation (GIGO) was signed 14 December 2023 and ratified through 2024. GIGO contracts a single industrial joint venture — Edgewing Limited — whose partners are BAE Systems (UK), Leonardo S.p.A (Italy) and Japan Aircraft Industrial Enhancement Co., Ltd (JAIEC). Engine work is delivered through Rolls-Royce/IHI/Avio. Only confirmed industrial announcements and government documents are recorded here.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: Between £10–£12 billion disclosed in UK National Shipbuilding Strategy / combat air budget lines through 2032. Final lifetime cost not yet disclosed.

contractors.groupValueNote: Tri-national intergovernmental organisation established under the GCAP Convention of 14 December 2023, headquartered in Reading, UK. Executive and programme contracts run through GIGO to Edgewing.

members.name: GCAP International Government Organisation (GIGO)
members.scope: Intergovernmental customer organisation (UK/Italy/Japan) for the Global Combat Air Programme. First Chief Executive: Oscar Nalesso (Italy) confirmed in gov.uk statement December 2024.

projects.researchNote: AUKUS is the trilateral security partnership between Australia, the UK and USA announced 15 September 2021. Pillar 1 delivers a new class of nuclear-powered attack submarines — SSN-AUKUS — to replace the UK's Astute class and Australia's Collins class. SSN-AUKUS will be built in Barrow-in-Furness (UK) and Osborne (Australia) from the 2030s. On 1 October 2024 MOD announced £3.95 billion of early AUKUS contracts with BAE Submarines and Rolls-Royce Submarines. Pillar 2 covers advanced capabilities (AI, quantum, undersea and hypersonic) with smaller distributed contracts.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £3.95 billion early design and long-lead contracts signed October 2024. Full build programme multiple tens of billions across three nations.

projects.researchNote: Skynet 6 replaces the legacy Skynet 5 satellites from 2027 onwards. Main elements: Skynet 6A (single bridging satellite); Skynet Enduring Capability (SKEC) — the long-term managed-service acquisition for Skynet 6B onwards; and the Secure Telemetry Tracking and Command (STTC) and ground-segment modernisation. Airbus Defence and Space holds a £500m Skynet 6A satellite contract signed 21 July 2020. SKEC is being delivered via multiple competitions from 2023–2025.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: Forecast c.£6 billion over the life of Skynet 6 per MoD announcements and NAO equipment plan coverage. Skynet 6A satellite alone is c.£500m.

projects.researchNote: The Wedgetail AEW Mk1 is the RAF's replacement for the retired E-3D Sentry. A £1.51 billion contract for five (later reduced to three) aircraft was signed 22 March 2019 with Boeing. Conversion of 737NG donor airframes takes place at Birmingham Airport, where STS Aviation Services holds the UK conversion sub-contract. Northrop Grumman supplies the Multi-Role Electronically Scanned Array (MESA) radar.

projects.contractingAuthority: Horizon Nuclear Power Wylfa Limited (project company)
projects.researchNote: Wylfa Newydd was a proposed 2.7 GW new nuclear station on Anglesey, Wales, using two Hitachi-GE Advanced Boiling Water Reactors. Horizon Nuclear Power was acquired by Hitachi in 2012. Hitachi suspended the project on 17 January 2019 and formally withdrew in September 2020 after failing to agree financing terms with BEIS. Hitachi reported c.£2 billion of pre-development spend in its Q3 FY2019 results. The UK Government purchased the Wylfa and Oldbury sites for £160 million in March 2024, now held for SMR deployment.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: c.£2 billion in pre-development spend by Hitachi across 2012–2019 (Hitachi FY2019 results, 7 February 2019).

projects.contractingAuthority: NuGeneration Limited (project company)
projects.researchNote: Moorside was a proposed three-reactor (3.4 GW) new nuclear station in Cumbria. Led by NuGeneration Limited — a JV originally between Iberdrola, GDF Suez and SSE. Toshiba acquired full control in 2014 through its Westinghouse subsidiary. The programme was built around the Westinghouse AP1000 design. After Toshiba put Westinghouse into US Chapter 11 in March 2017, the project stalled; Kepco (Korea Electric Power Corp) negotiated as preferred bidder but no deal emerged. Toshiba formally wound up NuGeneration in November 2018. Industry estimates of sunk costs are c.£450 million.
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: c.£450 million estimated sunk cost — Toshiba/NuGen publicly reported on wind-up in November 2018.

projects.researchNote: Protector RG Mk1 is the RAF's successor to Reaper (MQ-9A). It is a certifiable variant of General Atomics MQ-9B SkyGuardian. MOD announced an initial 16-aircraft £195 million Protector Release One contract (29 July 2020); the total planned fleet is 16 (with options) for c.£900m over life. projects.researchNote: The Future Strategic Tanker Aircraft (FSTA) is a 27-year Private Finance Initiative signed 27 March 2008 with AirTanker Services Limited for 14 Airbus A330 Voyager aircraft. FSTA provides RAF air-to-air refuelling, air transport and aero-medical evacuation. NAO HC 397 (30 March 2010) reported the contract's NPV at £10.5 billion over 27 years. AirTanker Services is a consortium (formally AirTanker Limited and AirTanker Services Limited) of Cobham, EADS/Airbus Group, Rolls-Royce, Thales and VT Group (now Babcock).
projects.programmeAggregateValueNote: £10.5 billion NPV of 27-year PFI contract (NAO HC 397, 30 March 2010).

contractors.groupValueNote: AirTanker Limited owns the fleet; AirTanker Services Limited operates the service. Both are JVs of the five consortium members.

projects.department: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government
projects.contractingAuthority: MHCLG (formerly DLUHC), with Homes England and Greater London Authority as delivery partners
projects.programmeValueNote: Building Safety Fund is part of a wider ~£5.1bn government cladding remediation portfolio reviewed by NAO in October 2024 (HC 267). NAO estimated total cost to fix unsafe cladding on all >11m buildings in England at £16.6bn. The portfolio comprises (i) £200m Private Sector ACM Cladding Remediation Fund (2019), (ii) £1bn Building Safety Fund for non-ACM cladding on >18m buildings (March 2020) and (iii) Cladding Safety Scheme launched by Homes England in 2023 for 11-18m buildings.
projects.researchNote: Strict-sourcing pass restricted to programme-administration and named advisory roles. Per-building remediation contractors are not enumerated because individual freeholder procurements sit below primary-source threshold.

members.name: Homes England
members.scope: Delivery partner for Building Safety Fund (non-London private sector applications); launched and administers the Cladding Safety Scheme from 2023; operates the Building Remediation Hub platform. Handles fund applications, eligibility assessment, grant disbursement and contractor scheduling checks.

projects.contractingAuthority: High Speed Two (HS2) Limited
projects.programmeValueNote: HS2 Ltd confirmed (via disclosures surfaced October 2023) that £1.25bn had been spent on contractor / consultant work for the Phase 2 scope that was cancelled by Prime Minister Rishi Sunak on 4 October 2023 under the 'Network North' announcement. This file records recipients of that £1.25bn of sunk spend; no further Phase 2 works will be let.
projects.researchNote: Strict-sourcing pass. All four recipients below published their own HS2 Phase 2 wins on their corporate news pages or via gov.uk. Bechtel inherited the Phase 2b Development Partner role after CH2M withdrew in 2017. MWJV is the Mott MacDonald + WSP joint venture referred to by that name in HS2 Ltd disclosures.

projectValueHistory.label: HS2 Phase 2 — Phase 2a (WMC-Crewe) and Phase 2b (Crewe-Manchester / West Midlands-Leeds) — CANCELLED — programme-aggregate
projectValueHistory.note: HS2 Phase 2 write-off of £2.17bn following Oct 2023 cancellation. NAO July 2024 report and HS2 Ltd annual report identified: £713m impairment Phase 2a assets; £137m impairment Phase 2b West; £153m impairment Euston; £1.1bn uncapitalised costs declared lost; £95m remediation on unneeded construction sites; plus £592m on land and property purchases no longer required. Closedown estimated at a further £100m over three years (NAO).

projects.projectName: Edinburgh Trams (Phase 1a — Airport to York Place)
projects.department: Transport Scotland / City of Edinburgh Council
projects.contractingAuthority: Transport Initiatives Edinburgh Ltd (TIE) — wholly owned by City of Edinburgh Council
projects.programmeValueNote: Final outturn for Phase 1a was £776m against an original 2007 budget of £545m; scope cut from Airport-Newhaven to Airport-York Place to contain cost. Audit Scotland interim report (February 2011) and Lord Hardie's Edinburgh Tram Inquiry (report 19 September 2023) catalogued procurement and project management failures.

projects.department: Scottish Office / Scottish Executive / Scottish Parliamentary Corporate Body
projects.contractingAuthority: Scottish Office (1997-1999), then Holyrood Project Management (Scottish Parliamentary Corporate Body)
projects.programmeValueNote: Final outturn £414m against original 1997 estimate of £10m-£40m. Lord Fraser's Inquiry (SP Paper 205, September 2004) found £150m of the cost 'wasted as a result of design delays, over-optimistic programming and uncertain authority'. Procurement via construction management contract was cited as a central structural failure.
projects.researchNote: Strict-sourcing pass. Lord Fraser of Carmyllie QC's independent inquiry (SP Paper 205) and Audit Scotland's Management of the Holyrood Building Project (June 2004) are the primary sources.

Audit Scotland report 'The Queensferry Crossing' AGS/2018/1 (January 2018) is the authoritative contractor reference.

projects.contractingAuthority: Royal Liverpool and Broadgreen University Hospitals NHS Trust (merged 2019 into Liverpool University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust).
projects.researchNote: Replacement acute hospital on the existing Royal Liverpool site. Construction began under PF2 contract awarded to Carillion-led consortium 2013. Carillion entered compulsory liquidation 15 January 2018, halting construction. Contract terminated March 2018; DHSC procured Laing O'Rourke to complete the works. Opened September 2022, five years late. Primary NAO report HC 28 (June 2020) 'Investigation into the rescue of the Royal Liverpool Hospital'.

projects.researchNote: Upgrade to the Warrior infantry fighting vehicle (new turret, 40mm cannon). Prime contract awarded to Lockheed Martin UK in 2011 (value £642m growing to c.£1.3bn). Cancelled by Secretary of State on 22 March 2021 as part of the Integrated Review (Hansard HCWS871); the vehicles will instead be retired and replaced by Boxer. Cavendish Fluor Partnership contract terminated 5 years early (Aug 2019); Magnox Ltd now direct NDA subsidiary.

projects.department: Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government (formerly DLUHC).
projects.contractingAuthority: Homes England (the Homes and Communities Agency).
projects.researchNote: £11.5bn five-year grant programme (launched April 2021) to deliver up to 180,000 affordable homes in England. Delivery through Strategic Partnerships (majority of grant) and Continuous Market Engagement. NAO 'Meeting the government's long-term housing ambition' (HC 1100, 22 June 2022) and Homes England Annual Reports are the primary references.

contractors.groupValueNote: Strategic Partnerships account for c.£8.6bn of the £11.5bn AHP 21-26 programme (Homes England announcement, August 2021).

projectValueHistory.label: Affordable Homes Programme 2021–26 — programme-aggregate
projectValueHistory.note: AHP 2021–26 envelope £11.5bn (£8.6bn outside London administered by Homes England, £4bn in London via GLA). Per MHCLG press release 7 Apr 2021. Target 180,000 homes; NAO Jul 2024 Affordable Homes report flagged underdelivery risk.
projectValueHistory.gradeRationale: B-domain: gov.uk + promoted by corroborating A URL

projects.department: Department for Education.
projects.contractingAuthority: Department for Education (delivering through the Education and Skills Funding Agency).
projects.researchNote: 10-year programme announced June 2020 to rebuild 500 schools. Main contracts let through the DfE Construction Framework (2021–) (Lots 1/2/3 by region and size) and the DfE Modern Methods of Construction framework. NAO 'Condition of school buildings' (HC 1058, 28 Jun 2023) is the principal reference; subsequent RAAC crisis August 2023 accelerated the programme.

contractors.groupValueNote: £7bn 4-year framework with 1-year extension option (award notice published November 2021 on Contracts Finder).

projects.researchNote: Proposed M4 relief road south of Newport (the 'Black Route'). Cancelled June 2019 by First Minister Mark Drakeford on environmental and cost grounds following a three-year public inquiry (2016–2019). Inspector William Wadrup had recommended approval; the First Minister over-rode this recommendation.

projects.projectName: Police NLEDS (National Law Enforcement Data Service)
projects.department: Home Office (programme delivered with Police Digital Service and National Police Chiefs' Council).
projects.contractingAuthority: Secretary of State for the Home Department (Home Office).
projects.researchNote: NLEDS was commissioned to replace the Police National Computer (PNC) and Police National Database (PND) with a single modern law-enforcement data service. Prime delivery contract awarded to Leidos Innovations UK Limited in 2019 at ~£385m over 7 years; subsequent contract amendments and partner awards raised total committed spend materially above the original envelope against a 2025 go-live that has repeatedly slipped. NAO reported 68% budget overrun and 5+ year schedule slip; PAC session 2024.

projects.projectName: Great Grid Upgrade (National Grid Electricity Transmission RIIO-T3)
projects.department: Ofgem (regulator) with National Grid Electricity Transmission, SP Transmission and SSEN Transmission as regulated delivery entities.
projects.contractingAuthority: National Grid Electricity Transmission plc (lead); SP Transmission plc and Scottish Hydro Electric Transmission plc for Scottish projects.
projects.researchNote: Largest reinforcement of the GB electricity transmission network since the 1950s. RIIO-T3 framework (2026–2031) authorises ~£35bn investment across 80+ major HV projects including onshore overhead lines, substations and HVDC subsea links (Eastern Green Link 1–4, SSEN Transmission projects). Consumer-funded via Ofgem-set transmission charges. 2024 tier-1 construction framework awards named below.

West Lindsey District Council challenged by judicial review (permission granted August 2023 in West Lindsey DC v SSHD [2023] EWHC 2037); HO proceeded to site preparation but the site never accommodated residents. Site use cancelled by the incoming Labour government in September 2024. Approximately £50m spent against an original £30m budget — reported in PAC session 2024.

projectValueHistory.label: A358 Taunton to Southfields Dualling (cancelled) — programme-aggregate
projectValueHistory.note: A358 Taunton to Southfields cancelled 30 October 2024 Budget on value-for-money grounds. Pre-cancellation scheme cost range £250m–£500m (8.5 miles dualling between Southfields Roundabout/A303 and M5 Taunton). National Highways paused DCO submission Dec 2023. Route Protection now removed. Individual consultant sunk-cost figures not public.

projects.department: Department for Transport (delivered by National Highways).
projects.contractingAuthority: National Highways Limited.
projects.researchNote: Proposed dualling of ~13 miles of the A1 between Morpeth and Ellingham in Northumberland. Development paused during earlier RIS2 resets and ultimately cancelled in October 2024 by the incoming Labour government under the Road Investment Strategy review. FOI responses confirmed £68.4m development expenditure before cancellation; full scheme had been estimated at ~£290m.

projectValueHistory.label: A1 Morpeth to Ellingham Dualling (cancelled) — programme-aggregate
projectValueHistory.note: A1 Morpeth to Ellingham dualling cancelled 30 October 2024 Budget on value-for-money grounds (BCR 0.95). Sunk development spend £68.4m at cancellation (up from £67m in 2023). Full scheme estimated cost had risen from £290m (2014) to £300m and up to £500m pre-cancellation. DCO granted 24 May 2024, later revoked 8 August 2025 citing planning blight. 8 miles of new dual carriageway never built.

projects.department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ).
projects.contractingAuthority: Great British Energy Limited (publicly owned company).
projects.researchNote: Publicly owned clean-energy company established by the Great British Energy Act 2025. HQ in Aberdeen. £8.3bn capital over the Parliament announced in July 2024 Budget. Strategic partnership with The Crown Estate on seabed leasing for offshore wind. As at April 2026 the company is in start-up phase — governance and initial investment commitments in place but no public record of major construction or supply contract awards.

Phase I (storage ring and initial 7 beamlines) ~£263m, operational 2007; Phase II added ~15 beamlines ~£120m to 2012; Phase III added further beamlines ~£111m to 2021. Funded 86% by STFC / UKRI and 14% by the Wellcome Trust via Diamond Light Source Limited (the delivery SPV).

projects.department: Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).
projects.contractingAuthority: Secretary of State for Work and Pensions.
projects.researchNote: Not a procurement story in the conventional sense. NAO's 2024 investigation found DWP had accumulated ~£357m in Carer's Allowance overpayments between 2018-19 and 2023-24 because DWP's Carer's Allowance service did not act on earnings-alert data shared by HMRC. The 'cliff-edge' rule meant exceeding the earnings threshold by £1 forfeited the entire weekly allowance. Approximately 600 recipients faced criminal prosecution. Administration is in-house at DWP's Preston site on the legacy CA Computer System (CACS); there is no prime 'delivery contractor' responsible for the failure.

members.name: Department for Work and Pensions
members.scope: Owns Carer's Allowance policy and in-house administration (including the CA Computer System). Accountable body for the overpayments failure identified by NAO.

projectValueHistory.label: Carer's Allowance Overpayments (administrative failure) — programme-aggregate
projectValueHistory.note: NAO report HC 294 (published Dec 2024) — cumulative outstanding Carer's Allowance overpayment debt £251.7m at 31 March 2024, up from £150.2m at end FY2018-19. Between 32,500 and 60,800 new overpayment cases identified annually. Not a procurement spend: this is administrative-failure overpayment exposure owed back to HM Government. Eligibility 'cliff edge' (no taper) drives rapid accrual. DWP launched independent review October 2024 (Pickering review).

projects.department: Department for Transport (via the Airports National Policy Statement). Scheme itself is privately promoted by Heathrow Airport Limited.
projects.contractingAuthority: Heathrow Airport Limited (privately owned infrastructure operator).
projects.researchNote: Proposed third runway ('Northwest Runway') at Heathrow. Airports National Policy Statement (NPS) designated June 2018 after parliamentary vote. R (Plan B Earth) v Secretary of State for Transport [2020] EWCA Civ 214 (Court of Appeal) held the NPS unlawful for failing to take into account the Paris Agreement; decision reversed by the Supreme Court in R (Friends of the Earth) v Heathrow Airport Ltd [2020] UKSC 52 (December 2020). Scheme paused during 2020 COVID pandemic; Heathrow had reported >£400m in development expenditure to 2019. Heathrow is privately owned (Ferrovial exit announced 2023 — now owned by FGP Topco Limited, controlled by a consortium including Ardian, PIF, and CDPQ).

members.name: Heathrow Airport Limited
members.scope: Scheme promoter and applicant for the Northwest Runway expansion; privately owned airport operator.

valueHistorySchema.description: Every member with a disclosed figure carries a valueHistory array. Each entry is a dated, sourced datapoint for that member's contract value. The scalar contractValueGBP is retained and always equals the most recent entry (valueHistory[-1].valueGBP). Appending a new entry (uplift, settlement, cancellation write-off, etc.) updates the scalar and preserves the audit trail.

entry.asOfDate: ISO-8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) or null if unknown
entry.note: free-text context, e.g. 'Notice to Proceed value'
